answer:
Definition

Evaluation Metrics for GenAI solve the "how good is my LLM output?" problem. Unlike traditional software where tests are pass/fail, LLM outputs are subjective. Metrics like faithfulness, relevance, answer correctness, and toxicity provide quantifiable quality scores for automated testing of AI features.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the AI policy assistant is evaluated with 3 metrics: faithfulness (does the answer match the policy document?), relevance (does it answer the actual question?), and harmlessness (does it avoid suggesting policy workarounds?). A nightly eval suite runs 100 test cases and alerts if any metric drops below threshold.

Code Example

// Evaluation framework for AI policy assistant
async function evaluateAssistant() {
  const testCases = await loadTestCases(); // { question, expectedAnswer, sourceDoc }
  const results = [];

  for (const tc of testCases) {
    const response = await askPolicyQuestion(tc.question);

    results.push({
      question: tc.question,
      faithfulness: scoreFaithfulness(response.answer, tc.sourceDoc),
      relevance: scoreRelevance(response.answer, tc.question),
      correctness: scoreCorrectness(response.answer, tc.expectedAnswer),
    });
  }

  const avgFaithfulness = avg(results.map(r => r.faithfulness));
  const avgRelevance = avg(results.map(r => r.relevance));

  if (avgFaithfulness < 0.85 || avgRelevance < 0.80) {
    await sendAlert('AI eval scores below threshold', { avgFaithfulness, avgRelevance });
  }

  return results;
}

// LLM-as-judge for faithfulness
async function scoreFaithfulness(answer, sourceDoc) {
  const judge = await claude.messages.create({
    model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
    system: 'Rate if the answer is fully supported by the source document. Return a score 0-1.',
    messages: [{ role: 'user', content: `Source: ${sourceDoc}\nAnswer: ${answer}` }],
  });
  return parseFloat(judge.content[0].text);
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, the faithfulness score was consistently high (0.95+) but employees reported wrong answers. Investigation revealed the scoring was too lenient — it checked if words appeared in the source but not if they were used in the correct context. Manoj switched to an LLM-as-judge approach that evaluates semantic faithfulness, not just word overlap.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using only automated metrics without human evaluation — metrics can game themselves (high score but low actual quality).
Mitigation: Combine automated nightly evals with monthly human review of 50 random production responses.

Validation Strategy

  • Nightly CI eval: 100 test cases → assert faithfulness ≥ 0.85, relevance ≥ 0.80.
  • Monthly human eval: 50 production responses rated by HR team on 1-5 scale.
  • Regression detection: compare eval scores before and after prompt or model changes.

Definition

The top 3 Evaluation Metrics mistakes are: relying only on BLEU/ROUGE (word-overlap metrics) for generative tasks, not having a baseline to compare against, and evaluating on synthetic data instead of real production queries.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the initial eval used string similarity between the AI answer and a golden answer. But the AI's correct paraphrase "You have 12 sick days annually" scored low against the golden "Each employee receives 12 sick leave days per year." Manoj switched to semantic similarity scoring, boosting eval reliability.

Code Example

// ❌ BAD: Word overlap scoring
function bleuScore(candidate, reference) {
  // Counts matching n-grams — penalizes valid paraphrases
  return calculateNGramOverlap(candidate, reference);
}

// ✅ GOOD: Semantic similarity + LLM judge
async function semanticScore(candidate, reference) {
  // Embedding similarity
  const embA = await embed(candidate);
  const embB = await embed(reference);
  const similarity = cosineSimilarity(embA, embB);

  // LLM judge for nuanced evaluation
  const judge = await claude.messages.create({
    model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
    messages: [{
      role: 'user',
      content: `Rate semantic equivalence (0-1):\nA: "${candidate}"\nB: "${reference}"`,
    }],
  });

  return {
    embeddingSimilarity: similarity,
    judgeScore: parseFloat(judge.content[0].text),
    combined: (similarity + parseFloat(judge.content[0].text)) / 2,
  };
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, eval scores improved after a prompt change, but production user satisfaction dropped. The eval test set was synthetic (written by the developer) and didn't include messy real queries like "can i take leave tmrw my kid is sick?" Manoj replaced the test set with 100 sampled production queries, revealing the prompt change broke handling of informal language.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Running evals only once during development — model behavior can drift with API updates or prompt changes.
Mitigation: Run eval suite in CI on every prompt change and weekly on a schedule to catch model-side drift.

Validation Strategy

  • Eval test set: 100 production-sampled queries with human-labeled expected answers.
  • Baseline tracking: store eval scores per version → chart trends over time.
  • A/B eval: compare new prompt against current prompt on the same test set before deploying.

Definition

The top 3 PII Redaction mistakes are: relying on regex alone (misses context-dependent PII), redacting in the response but not in the prompt (PII still sent to the LLM provider), and not handling PII in structured data (JSON fields, database columns).

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, employee names and IDs are sent to Claude for policy queries. Initially, full names and emails were included in the prompt — stored in Anthropic's logs. Manoj implemented a PII redaction layer that replaces identifiers with tokens ([EMPLOYEE_1]) before the API call and maps them back in the response.

Code Example

// PII redaction middleware
const PII_PATTERNS = {
  email: /[\w.-]+@[\w.-]+\.\w+/g,
  phone: /\+?\d[\d\s-]{8,}/g,
  aadhaar: /\d{4}\s?\d{4}\s?\d{4}/g, // Indian ID
};

function redactPII(text) {
  const map = new Map(); // token → original
  let redacted = text;
  let counter = 0;

  for (const [type, pattern] of Object.entries(PII_PATTERNS)) {
    redacted = redacted.replace(pattern, (match) => {
      const token = `[${type.toUpperCase()}_${++counter}]`;
      map.set(token, match);
      return token;
    });
  }

  return { redacted, map };
}

function restorePII(response, map) {
  let restored = response;
  for (const [token, original] of map.entries()) {
    restored = restored.replace(token, original);
  }
  return restored;
}

// Usage in API call
const { redacted, map } = redactPII(userMessage);
const aiResponse = await callLLM(redacted);
const finalResponse = restorePII(aiResponse.text, map);

Debugging Story

In the HR system, the redaction caught email addresses but missed employee names because names don't follow a regex pattern. Manoj added a name lookup table from the employee database — before redaction, known employee names are matched and replaced with [EMPLOYEE_N] tokens. This reduced PII leakage to the LLM by 95%.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Redacting PII in the prompt but the LLM hallucinates real-sounding names/emails in its response — different PII, but still a privacy issue.
Mitigation: Post-process LLM responses through the same PII scanner. Flag any PII patterns in the output before returning to the user.

Validation Strategy

  • PII detection test: 50 messages with known PII → assert 100% detection rate.
  • Roundtrip test: redact → call LLM → restore → assert original PII is correctly restored.
  • Audit log: log (anonymized) PII detection events for compliance reporting.

Definition

PII Redaction is the process of detecting and removing personally identifiable information (names, emails, phone numbers, ID numbers) from text before sending it to an LLM. This protects user privacy, ensures compliance with data protection regulations (GDPR, India's DPDP Act), and prevents PII from being stored in third-party LLM provider logs.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, when an employee asks the AI assistant "Can Ramesh Kumar (ramesh@visionquest.com) transfer his leave to me?", the PII layer redacts it to "Can [EMPLOYEE_1] ([EMAIL_1]) transfer his leave to me?" before sending to Claude. The response uses the same tokens, which are restored to real values before showing to the user.

Code Example

// Comprehensive PII redactor
class PIIRedactor {
  constructor(employeeNames = []) {
    this.nameSet = new Set(employeeNames.map(n => n.toLowerCase()));
    this.map = new Map();
    this.counter = 0;
  }

  redact(text) {
    let result = text;

    // Redact known employee names
    for (const name of this.nameSet) {
      const regex = new RegExp(name, 'gi');
      result = result.replace(regex, () => this.token('NAME'));
    }

    // Redact emails, phones, IDs
    result = result.replace(/[\w.-]+@[\w.-]+\.\w+/g, () => this.token('EMAIL'));
    result = result.replace(/\+?91[\s-]?\d{10}/g, () => this.token('PHONE'));

    return result;
  }

  token(type) {
    const tok = `[${type}_${++this.counter}]`;
    return tok;
  }

  restore(text) {
    let result = text;
    for (const [tok, original] of this.map.entries()) {
      result = result.replaceAll(tok, original);
    }
    return result;
  }
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, the PII redactor replaced "Manoj" in the query but also replaced "Manoj" in the policy document context ("Contact HR Manager Manoj for exceptions"). This confused the LLM because it couldn't match the policy's reference. Fix: Manoj scoped redaction to user input only, not the system-provided policy context — the policy is a controlled document, not user-generated PII.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Over-redacting common words that happen to match employee names (e.g., "Joy" as a name vs "joy" as an emotion).
Mitigation: Use case-sensitive matching for names, and only redact when the word appears as a standalone token (word boundaries).

Validation Strategy

  • Unit test: 30 messages with various PII types → assert 100% detection.
  • False positive test: 20 messages with no PII → assert zero redactions.
  • Compliance audit: log all redaction events → quarterly review for completeness.

Definition

Model Fallback is a resilience pattern where your app automatically switches to an alternative LLM when the primary model is unavailable, slow, or returns errors. It's like having a backup generator — if Claude is down, fall back to a local model or a cached response rather than showing users an error.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the AI assistant calls Claude Sonnet as the primary model. If the API returns a 5xx error or latency exceeds 5 seconds, the system falls back to Claude Haiku (faster, cheaper). If both fail, it returns a pre-cached answer from a FAQ database.

Code Example

// Model fallback with timeout
async function askWithFallback(question, context) {
  const models = [
    { id: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514', timeout: 5000, label: 'primary' },
    { id: 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001', timeout: 3000, label: 'fallback' },
  ];

  for (const model of models) {
    try {
      const controller = new AbortController();
      const timer = setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), model.timeout);

      const response = await claude.messages.create({
        model: model.id,
        messages: [{ role: 'user', content: question }],
        signal: controller.signal,
      });

      clearTimeout(timer);
      return { answer: response.content[0].text, model: model.label };
    } catch (err) {
      console.warn(`${model.label} failed: ${err.message}`);
      continue;
    }
  }

  // Final fallback: cached FAQ
  const cached = await faqCache.findBestMatch(question);
  return { answer: cached?.answer || 'Please contact HR directly.', model: 'cache' };
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, the fallback to Haiku worked but returned less detailed answers, which employees complained about. Manoj added a response-quality check: if the Haiku response is shorter than 50 words for a complex question, it appends "For more details, please contact HR" — setting expectations about the degraded response quality.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: All fallback models sharing the same API provider — if Anthropic has an outage, all Claude models fail together.
Mitigation: Include a non-API fallback (FAQ cache, local model) as the last tier. Monitor API health and switch proactively, not just reactively.

Validation Strategy

  • Chaos test: simulate primary model 503 → assert fallback activates within 5s.
  • Quality comparison: run 50 queries on primary and fallback → measure quality delta.
  • Latency budget test: assert total response time stays under 8s even with fallback.

Definition

Model Fallback solves the availability problem. LLM APIs have rate limits, outages, and variable latency. Without fallback, a single API error crashes your AI feature. With fallback chains, your app degrades gracefully — the user always gets an answer, even if it's from a simpler model or cached response.

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the AI tag-suggestion feature uses Claude Sonnet. During peak hours, API latency spikes to 8+ seconds — unacceptable for a UI interaction. The fallback chain switches to Haiku (sub-1s response) when Sonnet exceeds 3 seconds, and falls back to a keyword-extraction algorithm (no API) if both models are slow.

Code Example

// Tiered fallback with quality tracking
async function suggestTagsWithFallback(notes) {
  // Tier 1: Full AI model
  try {
    const tags = await withTimeout(
      () => suggestTagsWithSonnet(notes),
      3000
    );
    metrics.record('tag_model', 'sonnet');
    return tags;
  } catch (e) { /* continue */ }

  // Tier 2: Fast AI model
  try {
    const tags = await withTimeout(
      () => suggestTagsWithHaiku(notes),
      2000
    );
    metrics.record('tag_model', 'haiku');
    return tags;
  } catch (e) { /* continue */ }

  // Tier 3: Local keyword extraction (no API)
  metrics.record('tag_model', 'local');
  return extractKeywordsLocally(notes);
}

function withTimeout(fn, ms) {
  return Promise.race([
    fn(),
    new Promise((_, reject) =>
      setTimeout(() => reject(new Error('Timeout')), ms)
    ),
  ]);
}

Debugging Story

In ContactsHub, the Haiku fallback returned raw JSON with markdown fences while Sonnet returned clean JSON. The frontend broke when parsing Haiku's response. Manoj added a response normalization layer that strips markdown fences and validates JSON structure regardless of which model responded.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Not tracking which model served each request — you can't measure quality degradation or know how often fallbacks are triggered.
Mitigation: Log the model tier for every request. Dashboard alert if fallback rate exceeds 10%.

Validation Strategy

  • Chaos test: block primary model network → assert fallback activates.
  • Latency test: simulate 10s primary delay → assert response within 3s via fallback.
  • Quality parity test: compare tag quality across all 3 tiers for 30 contacts.
